coredata.dc:description: Using lithium-ion batteries requires an understanding of their deformation and failure under mechanical abuse. Properties of batteries under through-thickness loading has been the subject of various studies over recent years, however, in-plane properties are not yet fully understood. In-plane loading can lead to buckling, short circuit, or failure of connecting tabs. In this research, parameters controlling the in-plane deformation of cells are investigated, first by an analytical approach, then by a computational model of the cell’s Representative Volume Element (RVE). Analytical results show that cell lengths larger than the buckling wavelength do not affect the buckling strength thus allowing experimental results on a small specimen of the cell to be used for buckling analysis instead of expensive full cell testing. Computational models show that the RVE can be used to extract homogenized anisotropic material properties of full cells. Models of full cells calibrated from the analytical estimates and RVE were validated against experiments and show the efficiency of the approach, providing a tool for simulation of battery failures and designing efficient protective structures.

head.ce:title: Deformation of lithium-ion batteries under axial loading: Analytical model and Representative Volume Element

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using lithium-ion batteries requires an understanding of their deformation and failure under mechanical abuse. Properties of batteries under through-thickness loading has been the subject of various studies over recent years, however, in-plane properties are not yet fully understood. In-plane loading can lead to buckling, short circuit, or failure of connecting tabs. In this research, parameters controlling the in-plane deformation of cells are investigated, first by an analytical approach, then by a computational model of the cell’s Representative Volume Element (RVE). Analytical results show that cell lengths larger than the buckling wavelength do not affect the buckling strength thus allowing experimental results on a small specimen of the cell to be used for buckling analysis instead of expensive full cell testing. Computational models show that the RVE can be used to extract homogenized anisotropic material properties of full cells. Models of full cells calibrated from the analytical estimates and RVE were validated against experiments and show the efficiency of the approach, providing a tool for simulation of battery failures and designing efficient protective structures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: The global demand for electric vehicles has emphasized the need for more reliable lithium-ion batteries with higher capacities and higher efficiency. Researchers have studied the multi-scale and multi-physics features of such batteries including their thermal behavior and electrochemical characteristics (Tarascon and Armand, 2010; Abada et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018, 2020; Kim et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2018b; Li et al., 2021). One of the safety concerns associated with lithium-ion batteries is the possibility of failure, internal short circuit, and thermal runaway, in the case of mechanical abusive loading and damage. Such cases were seen from the widely reported Tesla Model S accidents and Samsung Galaxy Note recalls (Anon, 2019, 2017). Therefore, it is important to be able to predict the mechanisms and the extent of damage to the lithium-ion batteries in the case of excessive mechanical loads. This can be achieved by characterizing the mechanical properties and developing computational models of the cells under various loading conditions. Such models can provide insights into the response and failure of the batteries at both cell and component levels and can be utilized to detect the onset of an internal short circuit. The models can also be extended to characterize the response of the battery modules and packs and improve the design of protective structures to ensure safe application of such cells under mechanical abuse loading scenarios.

Lithium-ion batteries are available in various form factors: cylindrical, prismatic, pouch, and elliptical shapes. However, the layered structure of all form factors can be described by a similar building block: electrode layers coated on aluminum and copper foils which are separated by a porous polymeric film, namely the separator, see Fig. 1. Recent studies have extensively investigated the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery cells for different form factors (cylindrical (Sahraei et al., 2012a; Wierzbicki and Sahraei, 2013; Wang et al., 2020; Hollinger et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2015; Gilaki and Avdeev, 2016; Xu et al., 2016; Raffler et al., 2017; Xi et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2014; Sheikh et al., 2020), prismatic (Wang et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2020; Berg et al., 2020; Lyu et al., 2019; Kotter et al., 2019; Szabo et al., 2020), pouch (Breitfuss et al., 2013; Sahraei et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2017; Lian et al., 2020), and elliptical (Kermani and Sahraei, 2017; Kisters et al., 2017; Kermani and Sahraei, 2019; Kermani et al., 2019)) with special focus on the modeling of damage and failure under lateral loading. Mechanical response and failure of cell components have been investigated and reported in several publications (Zhang et al., 2016b; Dixon et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2018a; Sheidaei et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2016a). The in-plane (axial) loading scenario has received much less attention and has been investigated using only detailed layered simulations (Lai et al., 2014b; Ali et al., 2013; Lai et al., 2014a; Ali et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2016). However, in order to ensure that a full cell model is useful for the battery or automotive industry, it is essential to understand the response of the cell and the controlling factors, in both through-thickness and in-plane loading scenarios. Furthermore, to implement a cell model in full vehicle simulations, it should be computationally manageable. Kermani et al. (2019) demonstrated that a homogenized anisotropic model of an elliptical battery cell can be used to simulate both loading conditions. However, in that study, the fundamental mechanisms of the anisotropy and the extent of effects of cell parameters on the anisotropic response of the cell were not investigated (Kermani et al., 2019).

In order to obtain the homogenized response of batteries under in-plane loading, specially designed fixtures are required to confine full cells in a representative environment. Such fixtures should be designed and made accurately for each cell geometry, which entails significant cost. Also, such tests need to be repeated with any change in the electrode/separator assembly, as these tests do not provide information on the factors that affect cell anisotropy. Sahraei et al. (2012) used a special fixture to conduct confined in-plane compression tests on a small commercially available LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell in the quasi-static regime (Sahraei et al., 2012b). A comparison of the mechanical response of the cell under in-plane loading condition with that from through-thickness compression demonstrated the anisotropic behavior of the cell. The stress–strain curve from in-plane confined compression test was parallel to that of through-thickness tests, but about 9 MPa higher at each data point. In the case of in-plane compression test in the width direction, sixteen half wave buckles were developed in the cell (Fig. 2a). Two regions can be identified in the corresponding stress–strain curves (Fig. 2b). In the initial pre-buckling region, the stress–strain relationship is almost linear, and stress reaches a peak value of about 9 MPa at the strain of about 0.02. At this point, there is a sharp change in the slope and a transition from a uniform compression to buckling occurs. The buckling strength was calculated by counting the number of half waves seen in the in-plane tests and using beam-on-foundation formulation. Similar trends were observed for LiFePO4/graphite cell and module representative volume element specimens by Lai et al. (2014b). The results of these studies show that the in-plane mechanical response of cells can be predicted by adding the buckling strength of metallic layers to the through-thickness strength. However, these studies did not provide a tool to understand the factors affecting the buckling strength and to calculate the buckling strength without conducting the costly in-plane experiments.

In the current study, we use a three-step method to understand the deformation of lithium-ion cells under axial loading: an analytical analysis of factors affecting the buckling strength Section 2, a finite element (FE) simulation at the microscale level using Representative Volume Element (RVE) to validate the analytical method and improve the estimate of the axial response Section 3.1, and a macroscale homogenized anisotropic model to validate the previous two steps at the cell level Section 3.2. The proposed methodology is used to calibrate the anisotropic material behavior of elliptical cells and Section 4.

The analytical approach proposed in this study utilizes the through-thickness tests at cell and component levels in combination with plate-on-foundation theories to estimate the buckling strength of batteries under axial loading. This is an alternative method to in-plane testing of the cell which requires customized fixtures and handling of complex scenarios in movement of the fixture and in friction control. The analytical analysis also provides a tool to estimate the wavelength of buckling, which is needed as the smallest length that can be used for a cell RVE in multi-scale studies. One of the important factors for such studies is to make sure the RVE is repeatable, and if the size of RVE is smaller than one wavelength of buckling, this condition will not be satisfied. With having an RVE larger than the buckling wavelength, the need for a size effect study for computational homogenization is fulfilled.

In the next step of the study, a micro RVE was developed with the minimum length predicted in the previous step and by considering the properties of the components. This RVE model is capable of predicting the buckling strength with higher precision when compared with the analytical method and was used to validate the prediction of the analytical method in terms of buckling strength and wavelength. The RVE model is especially useful to predict the in-plane properties as a function of changes at component level. This is particularly valuable to battery manufacturers as they can change the properties of the components (such as thickness and material) and predict the cell response under various loading scenarios. The above two methods also provide a tool to estimate the in-plane properties for a homogenized cell material model, without the need for cell-level in-plane tests which are usually much more complex and costly when compared against through-thickness compression tests. We used the predictions of the developed analytical model and the RVE simulations to calibrate the anisotropic material models for pouch and elliptical battery cells and validated the results against experiments performed at the cell level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Buckling of a plate supported by unattached elastic foundation
ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are layered structures. In the case of in-plane compressive loading, each layer can be considered as a thin plate which is laterally constrained by its neighboring layers. In this section, we present an analytical approach to investigate the buckling of each layer by modeling the effect of neighboring layers as an elastic foundation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of analytical results with experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
Sahraei et al. (2012b) designed a special fixture and used it to conduct confined in-plane compression tests. As shown in Fig. 2a, the battery is fully confined between two flat plates that were carefully tightened by four screws and compressed by a flat punch form the top side. A peak force of 60 kN was recorded for this experiment. A total number of 16 buckles was observed in the direction of loading. The parameters of this small pouch cell were used for analytical calculations in the present study. The length ( L ) and width ( b ) of cathode and anode current collectors were 0.03 and 0.05 m, respectively, and the thickness of the cell ( h ) was 0.00535 m. The buckling analysis is conducted for a component layer in the center of the cell because this location is where the layers will have the maximum foundation support, and therefore highest critical buckling strength. We assume the component layer in the middle is supported by two half cells which have the average properties of the cell. These lumped average half cells provide the elastic foundation supporting the central component layer. With this assumption,
E
′
is the elastic modulus of the foundation and can be calculated as the slope of the stress–strain curve from through-thickness compression test performed on the cells. For the small pouch cell used here, the data up to strains of 0.015 were used and
E
′
was found to be 19 MPa (Sahraei et al., 2012b). The thickness of the elastic foundation is equal to half the thickness of the cell,
h
′
= 0
.00535/2= 0.002675 m and therefore
k = 7 . 1 ×
109 N/m
3
.

The buckling analysis was performed for each individual component in the cell. The component being analyzed was treated as a plate and the rest of the cell was the lumped elastic foundation. The following parameters were needed for such analysis: the elastic moduli of each component
( E )
, the thickness of each component
( t )
, and number of layers
( n )
. Table 1 lists these variables for the small pouch cell. The elastic moduli of the anode and cathode coatings and the separator were taken from the initial slope of the stress–strain curves from flat compression tests conducted at the component level (Deng et al., 2018). Eqs. (6)–(10) were used to calculate the buckling stress of the cell. It should be noted that the isotropic assumption is valid for each of the individual components in the cell. Therefore, the compressive through-thickness elastic modulus of each component is assumed to be equal to its in-plane value.

The results of the buckling analysis for cathode and anode layers are provided in Table 1. It can be observed that the buckling of the anode layer results in the lowest buckling stress for the cross section. Therefore, the anode layer is the first layer that starts to buckle during in-plane loading and will lead to buckling of all other components. This buckling stress is about 13.84 MPa and leads to number of half-waves being equal to 25. This corresponds to a buckling wavelength of
λ = 2 . 4 ×
10−3 m. The analytical solution over predicts the experimental critical stress of about 9 MPa and number of half-waves of 16. This was expected since our analytical solution does not account for experimental imperfections, while studies on battery cross-sections and the testing of layers of batteries show that the thickness and strength can be variable in different locations of the components and the cell due to effects of calendaring and other geometrical imperfections (Sahraei et al., 2015, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: A parametric study on factors controlling buckling of the cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical calculations were used to investigate the effects of cell size and properties on its buckling behavior (Fig. 3). For this purpose, the reference dimensions are
L = 0 . 03
m,
b = 0 . 05
m, and
h = 0 . 00535
m. It can be seen that the buckling stress and wavelength are independent of the cell length, as the variation of
σ
c e l l
(in Fig. 3a) is between 13.82 MPa and 13.86 MPa. Also, variation of λ is between 2.4 × 10−3 m and 2.5 × 10−3 m. This is not directly obvious from Eqs. (7) and (10) because of the dependency of m on L . This suggests that in-plane compression tests on a specimen cut from a large cell can give predictions on the behavior of the full cell. However, care should be taken to ensure that the minimum length of the specimen for testing is larger than the wavelength of the buckling, which can be determined by the analytical calculations. Furthermore, this means that in numerical simulations of lithium-ion batteries, an RVE can be modeled to study the response of the whole cell. This requires the RVE to have a minimum length equal to one wavelength to be able to represent the cell behavior.

By increasing the cell thickness, buckling stress decreases and the wavelength increases. The changes in buckling stress (
σ
c e l l
) are from 21 MPa for a 4 × 10−3 m cell to 0.17 MPa in cell thicknesses of about 100 × 10−3 m. This means that the difference between in-plane and out-of-plane stress levels decreases with an increase in cell thickness, implying that the effect of anisotropy is less pronounced for cells with larger thickness or for modules of batteries. The through-thickness elastic modulus has a substantial effect on
σ
c e l l
and λ . The increase in
E
′
(from 1 to 60 MPa) results in an increase in buckling stress (from 3.18 MPa to 24.58 MPa) and a decrease in wavelength (from 5 × 10−3 m to 1.8 × 10−3 m). This shows the most important factor in determining the buckling strength, under in-plane loading, is the elastic modulus of the full cell under through-thickness compression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro scale model of the representative volume element
ce:section.ce:para: LS Dyna software was used to develop a 2D microscale RVE model for the small pouch cell analyzed in the previous section (Sahraei et al., 2012b). Sahraei et al. (2016) used RVE modeling to investigate through-thickness compression and in-plane tension loading of lithium ion batteries (Sahraei et al., 2016). However, that model was not validated to predict in-plane compression of the RVE. The size effects become important for RVEs under in-plane loading because the wavelength of buckling controls the smallest repeatable unit for the cell. In this section, the objective is to develop an RVE that can be used to predict buckling load in the case of in-plane deformation. The calculations from the RVE will be used in the first step to validate predictions of the analytical model in terms of buckling wavelength and buckling strength. Then the buckling strength estimate from the RVE will be used to calibrate the homogenized response of the full cell in the next section. The ultimate validations of both analytical and RVE predictions will be performed by comparison of full cell model against full cell experiments under in-plane loading.

In this paper, the term representative unit (RU) is used to refer to a block consisting of two layers of separator, one layer of cathode, and one layer of anode (Fig. 4a). The size of the RU is 2.456 mm × 0.223 mm. RU length was chosen according to our analytical solution and is approximately equal to one buckling wavelength (see Section 2.2). This length is expected to allow for the formation of one full buckling wave and therefore fulfills the periodicity requirement. In the current study, the focus is the deformations in the cross section of the layers, rather than membrane displacements, therefore, a plane strain element formulation is selected. The model includes 1401 plane strain shell elements (ELFORM 13) with uniform lateral thickness of 1 mm. Thickness of each component is listed in Table 1.

For buckling simulations, the material property needed for each layer is the elastic modulus. The elastic moduli of the anode and cathode coatings as well as the separator were taken from the initial slope of the stress–strain curves from flat compression tests, as reported in Sahraei et al. (2012b), and are equal to 31, 105, and 95 MPa, respectively. The elastic moduli of the aluminum and copper foils are 70 and 110 GPa, respectively. Electrode coatings are merged with their corresponding current collectors. A 2D_AUTOMATIC_SINGLE_SURFACE contact with a coefficient of friction of 0.4 is defined for the interface between the electrodes and the separator. A constrained tied nodes failure criterion is implemented in the model to investigate if any fracture will be initiated as a result of formation of buckling waves. This failure criterion leads to splitting of the elements when the plastic failure strain is reached for each layer. A periodic boundary condition is applied to the RU in both directions using CONSTRAINED_MULTIPLE_GLOBAL. A uniform distributed compressive load of P is applied along the right edge of the model. LS Dyna implicit solver is used to predict the buckling stress. In such analyses, the buckling load can be calculated as the multiplication of the applied load P and the first eigenvalue.

In the first step, this model, consisting of one RU, was used to simulate the in-plane loading scenario. The deformation is shown in Fig. 4b, right. As can be seen in Fig. 4b, the formation of one buckling wave is captured, which verifies the wavelength calculations of analytical method. When using shorter lengths for similar simulation, for example the length used by Sahraei et al. (2016) for through-thickness compression, the simulation resulted in unrealistic deformation patterns in the model (Fig. 4b, left). The buckling stress calculated from this model is 14.71 MPa which is within 6% difference of the buckling stress of 13.84 MPa from the analytical solution. This amount of difference is acceptable because of the differences in assumptions and details included in these two approaches.

In order to improve the simulation of the foundation, consisting of the bulk of all surrounding layers, in the next step, a cuboidal RVE model was developed by extending the previous model to include 11 RUs. The size of the RVE is 2.456 mm × 2.453 mm. The buckling stress predicted by this model was 14.21 MPa which compares well with the analytical solution (error of 2.7%). The deformation is shown in Fig. 5a. In the last step, the model was extended to 21 RU which constitutes almost the total cell thickness. Buckling stress of 13.75 MPa was predicted by simulation which agrees well with the analytical solution of 13.84 MPa (Fig. 5b). Neither of the simulations resulted in failure in any of the layers of the assembly.

In order to examine the sensitivity of the FE model predictions of buckling stress when using RU, RVE, or full thickness simulations, four cases were considered for a parametric study, by changing the values of the elastic moduli of the anode and cathode coatings and the separator. Fig. 5c shows a comparison between buckling stress values predicted by each model. In all cases, the difference between RVE ( = 11 RU) and Full-Thickness ( = 21 RU) models are less than 4% which indicates that the RVE model can be used to successfully predict the buckling stress of the cell. The value of buckling stress calculated from the RVE simulation is ultimately used to calibrate a homogenized anisotropic material model for the full cell simulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation using macroscale model of the pouch cell
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the findings of the micro model and show application of the results to full cell modeling, a macro FE model of the small pouch cell was developed in LS Dyna. In this model, all the layers of electrode-separator assembly and the pouch cover is homogenized together in one part. This type of model, with large element size, is computationally viable for integration of battery cells into full vehicle models used by the industry to design and evaluate battery packs and protective structures. The dimensions of the cell in the current study were 0.034
×
0 . 060
×
0.00535 m. The element size was 0.001 m in both the machine direction (MD) and the transverse direction (TD) and was 0.486 mm in the through-thickness direction (TTD) (Fig. 6a). The model consisted of 22,440 nonlinear constant stress solid element (ELFORM = 1). The anisotropic homogenized response of the cell was modeled using MAT_MODIFIED_HONEYCOMB (MAT_126) material from LS Dyna library. The elastic modulus and shear modulus, in an uncompressed configuration, were set to 500 MPa and 227.3 MPa, respectively. This material model uses six independent input curves defining the stress–strain response in normal and shear directions in three principal directions of anisotropy. Furthermore, the curves have distinct behavior in tension versus compression. The compressive response of the TTD was calibrated based on through thickness compression tests (Sahraei et al., 2015). The in-plane stress–strain curve for the cell can be calculated by adding the buckling strength to the TTD hardening curve. The buckling stress calculated from the RVE, 14.2 MPa, was added to the through thickness stress–strain curve to estimate the cell response under in-plane compressive loads, i.e., in the MD and TD, see Fig. 6a. These hardening curves control the response of the honeycomb model before it reaches full compaction. Afterwards, when all the pores in the material are closed, the material follows a conventional elastic–plastic response. The relative volume, the ratio of the current volume to the initial volume, at which the cell is fully compacted is 0.70. This number corresponds to the porosity of the cell, which itself is a function of the porosity of anode, cathode, and separator layers ( ∼ 35%) in combination with metallic current collectors ( ∼ 0%). The Young’s modulus of fully compacted material is set to be 1 GPa. This stiffness is consistent with the elastic modulus reported from nano-indentation tests on anode coatings (Zhu et al., 2018c). The yield stress of the fully compacted material is assumed to be
σ
Y
= 180
MPa
, consistent with flow stress of metallic components of the cell (Zhu et al., 2018c). The cell was axially compressed with a rigid flat plate which moved downward uniformly. ERODING_SINGLE_SURFACE contact with a friction coefficient of 0.25 was used to model the interface between the moving plate and the cell. Two rigid walls were used to confine the cell in the through-thickness (out-of-plane) direction.

Fig. 6b shows the prediction of load–displacement for the model versus the experiment. It can be observed that the model predicts the initial jump in the curve (section A) until reaching the onset of buckling of the metal foils (point P). In the next section, the porous layers of anode and cathode coatings are undergoing compression and the gaps between the particles are being closed (section B). The force increases gradually following similar trends seen in through-thickness compression curves. In terms of stresses, this corresponds to the region where the stress–strain curves of through-thickness and in-plane directions are parallel. This is consistent with assumption of isotropic behavior for electrode coatings as they are the main component defining the response of the cell under through-thickness loading (Fig. 6b). At about 0.01 m displacement, there is a sharp increase in the slope of the curve which indicates the onset of densification. At this point, there is no more gap left, and the linear increase in force (section C) indicates elastic–plastic deformation of particles. The cell is fully compacted and behaves as a continuum solid rather than a foam. The location of this point corresponds to the relative volume of 0.7 given for full compaction of material. Section D exhibits a softening behavior pertaining to the plastic deformation of the fully compacted layers and potential damage in various components of the cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to another form factor: an elliptical cell with metal casing
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate applicability of the current research to other cell form factors, investigations continued by focusing on a small prismatic cell with metal shell casing, denoted in previous publications as an elliptical cell (Sahraei et al., 2016). In previous research of this team and collaborators, a 2D RVE model was developed for this cell and was subjected to through thickness compressive loads. However, the RVE size studied in that work was not fulfilling the size effect requirements for in-plane compressive loading scenarios. In this section, we estimate the wavelength of buckling for this elliptical cell using the analytical approach presented in Section 2 and then continue with finite element modeling of an RVE with the minimum length of one wavelength, as described in Section 3, to evaluate the in-plane strength of the elliptical cell. Subsequently, a macroscale model of the full cell is developed with its in-plane homogenized material properties calibrated from the analytical model and RVE simulation. This practice will further validate the concepts developed in the current study for another battery form factor with hard shell casing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro model of the elliptical cell
ce:section.ce:para:
The length, width, and thickness of the cell are
L = 0 . 063
m,
b = 0 . 035
m, and
h = 0 . 0145
m, respectively. Table 2 lists the detailed properties of the elliptical cell components. The elastic moduli of the cell components were determined from the initial slopes of the stress–strain curves under through-thickness compression loading (Sahraei et al., 2016). A through-thickness compression was simulated using the 2D RVE model, and the initial slope of the corresponding stress–strain curves was used as
E
′
, which was 65 MPa. In the first step, Eqs. (6)–(10) were used to estimate the buckling wavelength and the buckling stress of the cell. As shown in Table 2, the anode current collector is the component that buckles first. The analytical method predicts a cell buckling stress of 7.9 MPa and a number of half-waves of 76 which corresponds to wavelength of
λ = 1 . 66
×
10−3 m.

In the next step, an RVE micro model of the elliptical cell was developed. This length was equal to one buckling wavelength, 1.66 × 10−3 m, as determined from the analytical model. A rectangular RVE is developed with dimensions of 1.66 mm × 1.545 mm, which includes five RU (7005 shell elements). The implicit buckling analysis of the RVE micro model predicted the buckling stress of 11.4 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation using Full cell modeling of elliptical cell
ce:section.ce:para:
In the last step, the buckling stresses predicted by the analytical model (
σ
c e l l
= 7 . 9
MPa) and the RVE model (
σ
c e l l
= 11 . 4
MPa) were used to calibrate the homogenized material strength of a macro model of full elliptical cell. In previous studies, the axial response of this cell was calibrated by a generic estimates of buckling strength based on pouch cell experiments (i.e. shifting of the through-thickness stress–strain response by 9 MPa) (Kermani et al., 2019; Sahraei et al., 2016). In the current study, the homogenized anisotropic macro model of the jellyroll was updated to reflect the buckling strengths estimated in the present study to evaluate the importance and capabilities of the current predictions. The elliptical cell model contains a jellyroll developed using solid elements, homogenizing all the layers of electrode separator assembly into one smeared part. The anisotropic Honeycomb (MAT_126) model of LS Dyna was used for this part, as was done for the pouch cell in Section 3.2. The metal casing of the battery cell was modeled using a separate part with a piecewise plasticity material model (Fig. 7a). In the present simulations, the in-plane stress–strain curves of the jellyroll were recalculated by adding the buckling stresses predicted by the analytical model (
σ
c e l l
= 7 . 9
MPa) and the micro RVE (
σ
c e l l
= 11 . 4
MPa) to the homogenized through-thickness stress–strain curves. The elliptical cell was then simulated under axial compressive loading.

Fig. 7b shows the load–displacement response of the elliptical cell under in-plane compression, using four different calibrations: (i) an anisotropic model with in-plane calibration stress calculated by adding the buckling estimate (11.4 MPa) calculated from micro RVE to the stress–strain curve measured under through-thickness loading, (ii) an anisotropic model with the in-plane added stress calculated from analytical buckling model (7.9 MPa), (iii) a generic anisotropic model with in-plane calibration coming from tests on pouch cells reported in literature (9 MPa) (Sahraei et al., 2012b), and (iv) an isotropic model assuming through-thickness properties for all directions. An SAE 180 filter was applied to all simulation results. All models capture the initial region of the load–displacement curves up to displacements of about 2 mm, where the buckling of the shell casing starts to develop. For the isotropic model iv, the predicted force starts to deviate from experiments, at the displacement of about 1.75 mm, after which there is a dramatic decrease in load. All anisotropic models provide an improvement over the isotropic model and capture the overall behavior of the cell with respect to predicting the trend and magnitude of force. This highlights the necessity of using anisotropic mechanical properties to better predict the elliptical cell response under in-plane loading. Fig. 7b shows that the value of
σ
c e l l
affects the cell’s response after the buckling of the shell casing. Case i, which uses the buckling stress from the RVE model, provides the best match in prediction of force after buckling of shell casing when compared to the cell experiments. Cases ii and iii, calibrated from analytical model and generic literature data, slightly underpredict the force.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion on comparison of Pouch and Elliptical RVEs
ce:section.ce:para:
As explained above, the parameters needed for calibration of above models include the geometry measurements and the E (modulus of elasticity) values for all components. Geometrical measurements can be performed with a micrometer, and the E values can be measured by conducting lateral flat compression tests on each component and the full cell (or a subset of the full cell, as in a through-thickness RVE). A comparison is provided between the RVE models of the two cell types studied: the pouch cell and the elliptical cell (Fig. 8). The buckling wavelength was calculated by the analytical buckling solution of a plate on an elastic foundation for each cell (
λ = 2 . 456
×
10−3 m for the pouch cell and
λ = 1 . 66
×
10−3 m for the elliptical cell). In order to meet the periodicity requirement, these wavelengths were assigned as the minimum lengths for the representative units (RU). These detailed RU models were created by modeling each component separately. For this purpose, the internal structure of each cell (i.e., the thickness and the elastic modulus of each layer) were identified. As observed in tabular format in Fig. 8, the two cells are different in terms of the thicknesses and elastic modulus of each of the layers. These differences cause the variation in terms of the length of the RU and in terms of the number of layers required to form an RVE. Therefore, eleven and five RUs were combined together to create pouch and elliptical RVEs, respectively. These RVE models were used to predict the buckling stress of the cell (
σ
c e l l
), from the RVE simulations under in-plane compression. The anisotropic material response, in the in-plane direction, is obtained by adding
σ
c e l l
to the out-of-plane stress. For the case of the pouch cell,
σ
c e l l
predicted by the RVE simulation (14.2 MPa), is in good agreement with that from the analytical approach (13.8 MPa). However, in the case of the elliptical cell, the estimated value from the RVE (11.4 Pa) was slightly higher than what was found through the analytical calculations (7.9 MPa). These results highlight the importance of using the two-step analysis, 1- analytical modeling with bulk consideration of cell as the foundation for buckling of a component, which provides an estimate for the wavelength of the RVE and a first order approximation of the buckling strength, and 2- a micro-RVE model with detailed modeling of the surrounding layers, and with a length calibrated from previous step. Considering that the RVE model is including a more detailed representation of interactions between the layers, it is expectable that the values of buckling strength predicted from the RVE provides a closer estimate of the homogenized response of the cell, which was evident from the full cell modeling of the elliptical cell. Still the analytical model provides a good first order approximation of the values for quick calculations.

This manuscript provides a tool to understand the effects of various cell and component parameters on the structural anisotropy at the cell level. It also provides insights in selecting isotropic versus anisotropic material models when modeling lithium-ion batteries and modules. The importance of the proposed method is that the analytical and RVE models give insight on the effects of both structural and material properties of the constituent layers on the homogenized response of cells. Also, the provided tools reduce the number of tests required to calibrate the anisotropic cell material properties.

Effects of three variables on the anisotropic properties of the cell were studied and it was concluded that: (i) length of the cell does not change the buckling strength or the wavelength of the buckling. (ii) thickness of the cell has a substantial effect on the buckling strength and the wavelength. (iii) the most important factor affecting buckling strength is the through-thickness module of elasticity of the cell.

The present study provides two approaches to characterize the mechanical properties of large format cells in the in-plane direction, by adding the buckling strength of the cell to its through-thickness stress–strain relationship. In both cases, first an analytical model of in-plane loading is used to find the wavelength of buckling. (a) In the experimental approach, in-plane compression tests should be performed only on small specimens cut out of larger cells. The specimens should have a minimum length equal to one buckling wavelength as predicted by the analytical solution. This method eliminates the need to acquire bulky and expensive custom-made fixtures for confined axial compression of whole cells. (b) In the finite element approach, RVE models with a minimum length larger than the buckling wavelength (as predicted by the analytical solution) should be used to predict the buckling strength of the cell. Therefore, the anisotropic cell response can be found directly by adding the predicted buckling strength by either method to the measured unconfined through-thickness compression strength of the cell.

As a limitation of this study, it should be mentioned that the proposed method is applicable only to flat sections of pouch and prismatic cells and cannot be applied to cylindrical cells with wounded jellyrolls. Also, the post-buckling response of cells cannot be predicted by this approach.
